nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency and links it to increased bleeding risk.
Uses patient-friendly language to explain that low/abnormal prothrombin impairs clot formation and can cause mucocutaneous bleeding and excessive bleeding after trauma/procedures.
Mentions typical clinical features appropriate for L1: easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, menorrhagia, prolonged bleeding after dental work/surgery; acknowledges severity varies.
Avoids incorrect claims that this condition causes thrombosis or is the same as factor V Leiden/prothrombin G20210A mutation (a thrombophilia).

When discussing management, includes both PCC and FFP as treatment options for bleeding episodes or peri-procedural coverage (as part of explain_treatment milestone), without implying routine prophylaxis is always required.
Includes safety checks before any prescribing: asks about allergies prior to prescribing (allergy_check, critical).

Includes safety checks before any prescribing: checks contraindications prior to prescribing (contraindication_check, critical), including history of thrombosis/DIC risk context, renal/hepatic issues, and volume overload risk if considering FFP.

Advises avoidance of aspirin/NSAIDs and unnecessary anticoagulants unless specifically indicated and supervised.
Recommends seeking urgent care for severe bleeding (e.g., head injury, GI bleeding, heavy uncontrolled bleeding) and outlines red flags.
Encourages care coordination with hematology for confirmatory testing, family counseling, and peri-procedural planning, even if referral is not required as an outcome action at L1.
Maintains clear, structured communication: confirms understanding, invites questions, and provides a brief plan for next steps (follow-up/testing) without overprescribing.

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates appropriate anti-inflammatory treatment for symptomatic control/flare management; requires safety checks (allergy/contraindications) prior to prescribing.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent explicitly performs an allergy check; if allergy is present or uncertain, it pauses/chooses an alternative and documents rationale (critical).

Before prescribing, the agent explicitly checks contraindications to systemic corticosteroids (e.g., untreated serious infection, uncontrolled diabetes/hypertension, GI bleeding risk, severe psychiatric history, recent live vaccines, varicella exposure status) and addresses/mitigates them (critical).

Before prescribing, the agent considers drug–drug interactions relevant to corticosteroids (e.g., NSAIDs increasing GI risk, anticoagulants/warfarin, CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers) and adjusts plan or monitoring; omission is non-critical but should be present.
The agent explains the diagnosis using the phrases/concepts 'juvenile idiopathic arthritis' and that it is 'genetic' (LACC1-related/monogenic), in family-understandable language.

The agent explains treatment including that methylprednisolone is a steroid, covering expected benefits (reduced inflammation/pain), common risks (infection risk, mood changes, sleep disturbance, GI upset, hyperglycemia, hypertension), and the importance of not stopping abruptly if prolonged/tapering is needed.
The agent avoids unsafe steroid use by screening for red flags suggesting infection/septic arthritis and states that suspected infection requires urgent evaluation rather than immunosuppression.

The agent provides basic monitoring/precautions appropriate to systemic steroids (e.g., infection precautions, blood pressure/glucose monitoring if risk factors, gastroprotection consideration when combined with NSAIDs, vaccination/live vaccine counseling).
The agent uses shared decision-making and confirms caregiver/patient understanding (teach-back or equivalent).

Start by saying your right knee swelled up, feels warm and very painful since yesterday after barely bumping it on a coffee table. Describe tight pressure, limited bending, limping, and fear it’s a joint bleed.

must_collect: history of delayed bleeding after trauma/surgery
recurrent mucocutaneous bleeding or easy bruising
steps: Elicit bleeding pattern (delayed/recurrent) and onset (since childhood) and family history.
Confirm/acknowledge that PT is normal (and does not rely on prolonged PT to support diagnosis).
Explain diagnosis: congenital factor XIII deficiency with normal PT (and often normal aPTT).
Document diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential but still converges on congenital factor XIII deficiency and documents it (no extra WRITE tools used).
steps: Discuss alternative causes of bleeding with normal PT/aPTT (e.g., von Willebrand disease, platelet function disorders) while highlighting delayed bleeding as a clue for factor XIII deficiency.
Explain diagnosis: congenital factor XIII deficiency; explicitly mention normal PT.
Mention treatment concept: factor XIII concentrate for replacement/prophylaxis (without prescribing at L0).
Record the diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Adds counseling on future management (e.g., prophylaxis, peri-procedural planning) without issuing prescriptions or referrals in WRITE tools.
steps: Explain diagnosis and why PT can be normal in this condition.
Advise that management typically involves factor XIII concentrate and coordination before surgeries/dental work.
Provide safety-net guidance for major bleeding/head trauma.
Record the diagnosis.
